---
title: Hands-On Demo (Python)
subtitle: Reproducible and Dynamic Reporting with Python and Quarto
author:    
  - name: "Peter Degen"
    orcid: 0009-0004-0706-9200

  - name: "Gorka Fraga González"
    orcid: 0000-0002-1857-8607

  - name: "Fabio Molo"
    orcid: 0000-0001-9320-5854
    
  - name: "Samuel Pawel"
    orcid: 0000-0003-2779-320X
format:
  html:
    toc-depth: 4
    df-print: paged
  pdf: default
execute:
  eval: true
engine: jupyter
bibliography: references.bib
---

This hands-on demo requires Python and Quarto. Visit the [instructions](../../instructions/index.qmd) page to learn how to install them. We will further assume that you are using Quarto with VS Code (make sure you have the [Quarto extension](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=quarto.quarto)), although most of this demo is editor-agnostic.


# Creating a Quarto file

1)  In VS Code, create a new file with the .qmd extension
2)  Click on the button `Preview` on the top right corner of the editor or use the shortcut `Ctrl + Shift + K` to render a preview of the document
3)  An .html file with the same name as your .qmd file should have been generated

<h3>🎉 Congratulations, you have generated your first dynamic report! 🎊  </h3>

We will now modify the report with our own content.

# Editing the YAML header

The YAML[^1] header delimited by (`---`) specifies options and metadata of your report. Add information about `author` and `date` (use `last-modified` to always display the latest date) and define the output format as either `pdf` or `html`. Be careful with *indentation*, YAML is sensitive to spacing! The header can be kept simple or it can contain a lot of information. An example:

[^1]: YAML is an acronym for "Yet Another Markup Language"

``` yaml
---
title: "Title of the report"
subtitle: "Subtitle"
date: last-modified # display the date of the last edit
author: 
  - name: "Author 1"
    orcid: 1234-1234-1234-12345
    affiliation:
      - name: "University of Zurich"
        city: "Zurich"
  - name: "Author 2"
    orcid: 5678-5678-5678-5678
    affiliation:
      - name: "University of Bern"
        city: "Bern"
format:
  html:
    toc: true # add a table of contents
    toc-depth: 4 # show level 1-4 subheadings in the table of contents
  pdf: default
engine: jupyter
---
```

<!-- ::: {#tip-chunks .callout-tip collapse="false"} -->

<!-- #### Multiple output formats -->

<!-- When specifying multiple outputs, clicking on the down-arrow next to `Render` allows you to change the output format (e.g., change from html to pdf) -->

<!-- ::: -->

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Optional exercises**

-   Also add your email and affiliation
-   Render the report both as HTML and PDF
:::

Re-render your report to see the changes you just made.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
#### Render often

When working on a dynamic report it is recommended to re-render the report often. This allows us to quickly spot errors and fix them on the fly. A useful keyboard shortcut for rendering is `Ctrl+Shift+K` (or `CMD+Shift+K` on Mac).
:::

# Editing the report content

The two most important elements of a dynamic report are text and code.

## Text

Quarto uses Markdown syntax to structure and format text. For example,

``` markdown
# This is a title

Two stars will render a word **bold**. 

We can also create lists:

- Element 1
- Element 2

A useful link is this [Markdown guide](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html)

> An impressive quote
```

produces

<h2>This is a title</h2>

<!-- HACK to not have the title in the TOC -->

Two stars will render a word **bold**.

We can also create lists:

-   Element 1
-   Element 2

A useful link is this [Markdown guide](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html)

> An impressive quote

This type of text editing is different from WYSIWYG (*What you see is what you get*) editors, such as Microsoft Word. In VS Code, rendering a .qmd file creates a new window next to your source code that shows a preview of the formatted document and provides a WYSIWYG-like interface.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Optional exercise**

Include an image in your report.
:::

<!-- ![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/7/74/A-Cat.jpg){fig-align="center" width="273"} -->

## Code chunks

[Code chunks](https://quarto.org/docs/computations/python.html) contain executable code and let you embed results directly into your text (e.g., data summaries, plots, or tables). Code chunks are automatically re-run when the document is rendered. If any code chunk fails, the entire document may fail to render. This encourages writing reproducible, error-free code.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
#### Code chunk options

Code chunks are delimited by ```` ```{python} ```` (you can specify other languages like R, Julia, ObservableJS). For each code you can add *chunk options* to the code chunks with `#|`, for example `#| echo: false` will hide the code. In VS Code you can see the options available if you type `#| ` (note the whitespace) and then press `Ctrl + Space`. Many options can be set globally for the entire document on the YAML header (e.g., under `execute:`, under `html:`, or under `pdf:`).
:::

### Load libraries

We first load all Python packages that we will use in our analysis. Copy the code below inside a code chunk.

```{python}
#| warning: false
#| output: true
import pandas as pd # data wrangling
import itables # nicer tables
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # plotting
import seaborn as sns # nicer plots

sns.set() # activate seaborn defaults
itables.options.allow_html = True # needed to hide index with itables
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Optional exercises**

Explore different options for code execution:

-   Hide all <!-- echo: false -->
-   Print all code but do not execute it <!-- echo: true and eval: false -->
-   Fold all code (accessible by clicking on a button when rendered as HTML) <!-- code-fold: true --> <!-- -   Hide only the code that generates the table -->
:::

### Read the data

For this example we will use the data set "Vornamen neugeborener Mädchen und Knaben mit Wohnsitz in der Stadt Zürich" (<https://data.stadt-zuerich.ch/dataset/bev_vornamen_baby_od3700>). This data set contains the names of newborns in the city of Zurich since the year 1993. The data was kindly made available to the public domain via a CCZero license by Open Data Zürich. We will now import the data and rename the variables into English.

```{python}
url = "https://data.stadt-zuerich.ch/dataset/bev_vornamen_baby_od3700/download/BEV370OD3700.csv"
dat = pd.read_csv(url)
datclean = dat.rename({
  "StichtagDatJahr": "year",
  "Vorname": "name",
  "SexLang": "sex",
  "AnzGebuWir": "births"
  }, axis=1)
datclean.replace({"sex": {"weiblich": "female", "männlich": "male"}}, inplace=True)
```

### Summarizing the data

We first compute the most common name by year and sex.

```{python}
ix = datclean.groupby(['sex', 'year'])['births'].idxmax()
summaries = datclean.loc[ix]
summaries.sort_values('year', ascending=False, inplace=True)
summaries_styled = summaries.style.hide(axis="index")
summaries_styled
```

### Creating a table

We can create an interactive table summarizing our results using the [`itables`](https://mwouts.github.io/itables/quick_start.html) package.

```{python}
itables.show(summaries_styled, caption="Most common name of newborns in the city of Zurich by year and sex")
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Optional exercises**

-   Make a table that displays the number of registered births per year
:::

### Plot the data

Creating a plot in a code chunk automatically inserts it into the rendered Quarto report. For example, we may want to plot the most common names between 2021 and 2024.

```{python}
#| label: fig-names
#| fig-cap: "Most common names of Zurich newborns in 2021-2024."
#| message: false

top10 = (
    datclean.loc[datclean["year"] > 2020]
    .groupby(["sex", "name"], as_index=False)["births"]
    .sum()
    .sort_values(["sex", "births"], ascending=[True, False])
    .groupby("sex")
    .head(10)
)

g = sns.FacetGrid(top10, col="sex", sharex=False, sharey=False, height=5, aspect=0.75)
g.map_dataframe(sns.barplot, y="name", x="births")

# Add bar labels
for ax in g.axes[0]:
  ax.bar_label(ax.containers[0], fontsize=10, padding=-15, color="white")

g.set_axis_labels("Births", "Name")
g.set_titles(col_template="{col_name}")
g.fig.subplots_adjust(top=0.85)
plt.tight_layout()
```

Note that we added chunk options to create a caption (`#| fig-cap: "Most common names of Zurich newborns in 2021-2024."`) and a label (`#| label: fig-label`). We can then refer to [@fig-names] in the text using `[@fig-label]`.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Optional exercises**

-   Change the height and width of your plot
-   Add a label to the previously generated table and refer to it in the text (beware that table labels start with `tab` instead of `fig`).
:::

### Multiple figures

Quarto can also nicely display multiple figures simultaneously. For example, we may want to plot the occurrence of names over time in two plots. Here we choose Peter, Samuel, and Gorka for one figure and Daniel and Sarah for the other. 

```{python}
#| label: fig-names-over-time
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| fig-cap: "Popularity of names over time."
#| fig-subcap:
#|   - "Peter, Samuel, and Gorka"
#|   - "Daniel and Sarah"
#| message: false

# first figure
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(4,4))
first = datclean[datclean["name"].isin(["Peter", "Samuel", "Gorka"])]
sns.lineplot(data=first, x="year", y="births", hue="name", marker="o", ax=ax)

# second figure
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(4,4))
second = datclean[datclean["name"].isin(["Daniel", "Sarah"])]
sns.lineplot(data=second, x="year", y="births", hue="name", marker="o", ax=ax)
```

Since we specified the chunk option `layout-ncol: 2`, both figures are displayed next to each other. Of course, we could have also used Python to create one figure with two subplots using `plt.subplots(1, 2)`. Here, we chose to make two figures to demonstrate the `fig-subcap` chunk option.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Optional exercise**

Add a third plot with your own name!
:::

### Inline code

A "killer-feature" of dynamic reporting is the possibility to add [inline code](https://quarto.org/docs/computations/inline-code.html). This allows us to dynamically insert data summaries into text, avoiding tedious and error-prone copy-and-pasting. For example,

> In 2023, the most common male name was \``` {python} summaries.query("year == 2023 and sex == 'male'").name.iloc[0]` `` whereas the most common female name was \``` {python} summaries.query("year == 2023 and sex == 'female'").name.iloc[0]` ``.

produces

> In 2023, the most common male name was `{python} summaries.query("year == 2023 and sex == 'male'").name.iloc[0]` whereas the most common female name was `{python} summaries.query("year == 2023 and sex == 'female'").name.iloc[0]`.

Note that, instead of performing computations within an inline code chunk, it is often advisable to perform computations in a separate code chunk, save the results to a variable, and then print the variable in the inline code chunk.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Optional exercise**

Compute the total number of births in the data set and then display it via an inline code chunk.
:::

## References

In Quarto, it is also possible to include a bibliography file in BibTeX format (with .bib file extension) to automatically generate citations and a bibliography (<https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/citations.html>). This file has to be specified in the YAML header. In this demo, we have a `references.bib` file in the same directory as this .qmd file, so we specify:

``` yaml
bibliography: references.bib
```

We can then use `[@Hofmann2023]` (with parentheses) or `@Hofmann2023` (without parentheses) to cite a bibliography item named Hofmann2023. For example, @Hofmann2023 provides an introduction to dynamic reporting. @Peikert2021 summarizes a more advanced dynamic reporting workflow. @Knuth1984 proposed the literate programming paradigm.

A typical entry from a BibTeX bibliography file looks like this:

``` bibtex
@article{Knuth1984,
  title = {Literate Programming},
  volume = {27},
  DOI = {10.1093/comjnl/27.2.97},
  number = {2},
  journal = {The Computer Journal},
  author = {Knuth,  D. E.},
  year = {1984},
  pages = {97--111}
}
```

These files can either be created manually o with a reference manager (e.g., [Zotero](https://www.zotero.org/)).

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Optional exercise**

Cite one of your favorite papers
:::

## Reproducibility

When creating dynamic reports, it is good practice to document the software versions and other parts of the computational environment. In reports that use the R programming language, a simple yet effective way to document your computational environment is to include the output of `sessionInfo()`. While there is no built-in equivalent in Python, several workarounds exist. For example, the [session-info](https://pypi.org/project/session-info/) package provides similar functionality to R's `sessionInfo()`. Alternatively, you can also print the installed packages using a command from your Python package manager, for example, `!pip freeze` or `!conda list` (the `!` is used to execute shell commands directly from a Python chunk in a Quarto notebook). Yet another option is to manually print the computational environment using only the Python Standard Library:

```{python}
import sys
import platform
import importlib.metadata as metadata

print("Python version:", sys.version)
print("Platform:", platform.platform())
print("\nInstalled packages:")

for dist in sorted(metadata.distributions(), key=lambda d: d.metadata["Name"].lower()):
    name = dist.metadata["Name"]
    version = dist.version
    print(f"  {name}=={version}")

```

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Optional exercise**

Install the `session-info` package and use it to capture the computational environment for this report. Try also `!pip freeze` in your Quarto notebook. How do the outputs differ? Which option is more reproducible?
:::

# Practical tips

This concludes a first introduction to dynamic reporting with Quarto. Here are some further tips

-   Nobody knows all the options by heart, use the [Quarto documentation](https://quarto.org/docs/reference/)!
-   Re-render your reports often to spot errors quickly
-   Use relative paths so that others can reproduce your report
-   Always include the computational environment at the bottom of your report to show details on the computational environment (e.g., package versions) used to generate your report <!-- - When sharing your analyses, include a README file that documents all steps necessary to create your report -->

# Gallery (optional)

The are many examples of dynamically generated reports online. Explore the links below:

-   Example 1. Reproducible HTML report using R Markdown: <https://data.snf.ch/stories/open-research-data-2023-en.html>
-   Example 2. Reproducible PDF report using knitr: <https://gitlab.uzh.ch/fabio.molo/nhb-replication>
-   Example 3. Website with dynamically generated course materials: <https://github.com/avehtari/BDA_course_Aalto/tree/master>
-   Example 4. Reproducible HTML slides with Quarto: <https://github.com/kjhealy/flipbookr-quarto/tree/main?tab=readme-ov-file>.
-   Example 5. Code repository of a paper with multiple Quarto analyses (HTML): <https://github.com/SamCH93/missSim>

Reflect about the following questions:

-   [ ] Can you find the source files that generate the reports?
-   [ ] Which dynamic reporting tools are used?
-   [ ] Can you access the outputs?
-   [ ] Do they use some advanced features for dynamic reporting or computational reproducibility?
-   [ ] What would be the workflow to reproduce these reports?

# References
